content: Coffee consumption is associated with reduced risk of Alzheimer's disease and other neurodegenerative processes, but the effect may be more than a matter of caffeine.
Trigonelline has yet to be investigated in humans, but preclinical studies suggest it may have beneficial effects on cognition.
Observational studies investigating 'coffee' can refer to non-uniform beverages consisting of any number of relative concentrations of compounds.

Randomized, interventional studies may impose some uniformity on the coffee consumed, but the intrinsic heterogeneity of the beverage means no two trials will ever be exactly identical in their treatments.
Caffeine does not appear fully responsible for the 'magic' of a daily brew, and the effects of each individual component on cognition remain poorly understood.
Turning investigative attention toward coffee itself comes with its own headaches, largely reflecting the heterogeneity of coffee beans and preparation styles.
